one_line_takeaway: FUNPOISON shows that injecting side-effect-free, type-checked 'weak-use' code fragments into live (not dead) execution paths at just a 10% poisoning rate degrades unauthorized fine-tuning performance (delta-Pass@1 significantly negative) while preserving 100% compilability/executability, and this effect survives static analysis, formatting, LLM rewriting, and adaptive detector attacks that all defeat CoProtector's prior full-poisoning, functionality-breaking approach.
key_finding: At only 10% poisoning, FUNPOISON drives delta-Pass@1 clearly negative on DeepSeek-Coder-1.3B (consistent across decoding temperatures 0.0/0.2/0.4), while CoProtector's CSR shows no significant effect at any rate and CC/CR/CS only show impact at 100% poisoning. FUNPOISON simultaneously maintains 984/984 compilation success, 100% executability, negligible dynamic overhead (mean p95 time overhead 2.29%, p95 memory overhead 0.09%, identical 100% line coverage, near-identical execution jitter 8.17% vs 8.12% clean-vs-poisoned), and full functional correctness on Apache Commons Lang (191/1,908 functions poisoned, all 57,764 unit tests still pass). The mechanism analysis shows failed generations strongly co-occur with injected structural patterns (e.g., at 10%/T=0.0, 755/785 template occurrences and 415/435 weak-use occurrences co-occur with execution failures), and the DeadBranchInsertion ablation (same templates, but placed in dead code) at 10% poisoning matches clean fine-tuning (Pass@1 0.38) whereas FUNPOISON at 10% drops Pass@1 to 0.20 -- demonstrating the degradation comes from execution-path (live-code) placement, not mere template-text exposure. Robustness results: KillBadCode/DeCoMa purification only reduce effective poisoning rate to 7.9%/8.2% (from 10%) yet fine-tuned models on purified data still underperform base; LLM-based rewriting achieves only 0.07 (CodeLlama) and 0.06 (GPT-4) accuracy at removing FUNPOISON on 500 poisoned samples (vs. much higher removal accuracy on CoProtector's CC/CR/CS/CSR); CodeQL static analysis does not separate poisoned from clean samples; an adaptive CodeBERT detector trained under a favorable 50% poisoned setting collapses to 100% false-positive rate (10.39% accuracy); clang-format normalization does not remove the poisoning effect.
core_contribution: FUNPOISON, a functionality-preserving dataset poisoning framework that lets code dataset owners deter unauthorized fine-tuning of Code LLMs. It builds a reusable pool of statement-level templates mined from real code (extracted via statement extraction, made independently compilable via a javac-driven repair step, and annotated with identifier metadata for conflict-aware reuse), then injects them into a subset of the protected dataset through three controlled-injection stages: (1) a two-tier safety filter that removes any template with potential side effects (I/O, concurrency, process control, reflective/shared-state interactions), (2) type-aware 'weak-use' synthesis that generates statements which consume injected variables (boxing, identity hashing, guarded size checks) without altering observable behavior, and (3) execution-safe site selection that only inserts at syntactically/semantically inert seams within method bodies (excluding sites near control-transfer statements or with side effects). The paper also isolates the poisoning mechanism with a controlled ablation, DeadBranchInsertion, which uses the same template pool but places templates in always-false branches instead of the live execution path.
threat_model: Dataset owner releases code for ordinary development use but does not authorize large-scale model adaptation/fine-tuning. Attacker collects the released data and fine-tunes a pretrained CodeLLM without authorization; the attacker fully controls the training pipeline (model choice, optimization, data preprocessing, formatting, static analysis, purification, LLM-based rewriting) and may know FUNPOISON is in use, potentially building signature rules or supervised detectors. The attacker does not have access to a separately reserved clean version for authorized users, cannot manually inspect web-scale data, and must keep an acceptable false-positive rate when filtering (cannot afford to strip too much benign code). Defense success is defined as unauthorized fine-tuning failing to yield meaningful Pass@k gains over the base model, while benign/authorized users still get fully functional code.
limitations: Evaluated only on Java; other languages (C/C++, JavaScript, Go, Rust) would need language-specific weak-use pools, parsers, compiler checks, and side-effect filters. Empirical scope restricted to executable code generation -- other code tasks need separate evaluation. Insertion-site availability limits deployment: only 80.3% of CodeSearchNet Java functions admit valid execution-safe injection sites under full coverage, so highly compact/optimized codebases may be harder to poison. The method is explicitly not claimed to be theoretically unremovable -- only that the evaluated removal, rewriting, static-analysis, formatting, and adaptive-detection attempts fail to achieve a useful effectiveness/false-positive/semantic-preservation/cost trade-off; effectiveness under substantially different training regimes (aggressive curriculum learning, large-scale pretraining from scratch, RL-based adaptation) remains untested. Authors note dataset poisoning is dual-use and could disrupt legitimate training if deployed indiscriminately, and explicitly do not recommend default application to collaborative open-source ecosystems.

datasets_used: CodeSearchNet (CSN) -- 100K sampled Java functions for training data poisoning
HumanEval-X (Java subset, 164 tasks; 984 instances via six-fold duplication for dynamic/static analysis) -- primary evaluation benchmark
Apache Commons Lang -- real-world codebase (1,908 functions, 57,764 unit tests) for functionality-preservation validation
quantitative_results: delta-Pass@1 on DeepSeek-Coder-1.3B: significantly negative at 10% poisoning, more pronounced at 50%, consistent across temperatures 0.0/0.2/0.4
Table 2: Base Pass@1=0.31, Clean fine-tuned=0.38, FUNPOISON@10%=0.20, DeadBranchInsertion@1%=0.37, @10%=0.38, @50%=0.34, @100%=0.35

Table 3 dynamic analysis (984 tasks): compilation success 984/984 both clean and poisoned; mean p95 time overhead 2.29% (p95:25%); mean p95 memory overhead 0.09% (p95:2.41%); coverage 100% both; execution jitter 8.17% clean vs 8.12% poisoned; behavior fully preserved

Table 1 mechanistic evidence: 10%,T=0.0 weak-use fail/total=415/435, template fail/total=755/785; 10% all-T weak-use=1295/1363, template=2231/2344; all rates/all-T weak-use=5328/5653, template=8093/8810
Apache Commons Lang: 191/1,908 functions poisoned (10%), all 57,764 unit tests pass after poisoning
Table 4 rewriting removal (500 poisoned samples): FUNPOISON ACC=0.07 (CodeLlama) / 0.06 (GPT-4) vs. CC=0.25/0.68, CR=0.61/0.96, CS=0.23/0.18, CSR=0.50/0.50

Purification: KillBadCode recall 0.41, DeCoMa recall 0.54 at 10% poisoning rate, reducing effective poisoning to 7.9% and 8.2% respectively; purified-data fine-tuned models still underperform base model
Table 5: CodeQL detection -- same distribution as clean, Rule 32 flags 4.3%; CodeBERT adaptive detector (50% poisoned training split) FPR 100%, accuracy 10.39%

Table 6 (10% poisoning generalization): HumanEval-X CodeLlama-7B Pass@1 T=0.0 -- Base 0.29, Clean FT 0.31, FUNPOISON 0.23; CodeLlama-7B-Instruct -- Base 0.30, Clean FT 0.38, FUNPOISON 0.30; MBPP DeepSeek-1.3B -- Base 0.31, Clean FT 0.41, FUNPOISON 0.16
Insertion-site availability: 80.3% of CodeSearchNet Java functions admit valid execution-safe injection sites under full coverage
baselines_compared: CoProtector (Sun et al., 2022) -- four transformations: code corruption (CC), code splicing (CS), code renaming (CR), comment semantic reversal (CSR); the only prior untargeted code-dataset poisoning method
DeadBranchInsertion -- controlled ablation introduced by this paper, same template pool as FUNPOISON but placed in always-false (dead) branches
Detection/purification baselines: Spectral Signature, Activation Clustering, KillBadCode, DeCoMa
Static analysis baseline: CodeQL (33 Java rules)
Adaptive detector baseline: CodeBERT classifier trained under 50% poisoned setting
